refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported in real-time to the Site Manager and Equipment Operator.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement adds explicit measurement, reporting, and stakeholder visibility to transform a vague 'support achieving' into a verifiable, monitored outcome. The p95 percentile metric and duty cycle context are critical for acceptance criteria validation.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented field trials under representative duty cycle conditions; statistical analysis of cycle time samples (n ≥ 100 cycles) to confirm p95 ≤ CT_target; real-time telemetry dashboards showing cycle time distribution and trend analysis.
sub_frs: FR-2.1: Measure and timestamp each phase of the load→travel→dump cycle with ±0.1 s accuracy.
FR-2.2: Compute p95 cycle time statistic from a rolling window of recent cycles.
FR-2.3: Report cycle time KPI and performance alerts to Site Manager and Equipment Operator via real-time dashboard.
FR-2.4: Optimize vehicle motion control and teleoperation responsiveness to minimize cycle time variance and mean latency.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Integrate high-frequency inertial measurement units (IMUs) and GPS on the vehicle; stream raw telemetry to a central analytics engine that computes cycle time statistics and pushes alerts to operator dashboards.

DP-B: Embed cycle-time measurement logic directly in the vehicle's onboard controller; report aggregated p95 statistics via periodic telemetry packets to the Teleoperation system, which displays KPI on operator consoles.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach: vehicle-side event detection (load, travel start, dump) with timestamp logging; Teleoperation system aggregates events, computes p95, and maintains a real-time KPI dashboard accessible to Site Manager and Operator; Safety Services monitors for anomalies.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid cycle-time measurement and reporting architecture with vehicle-side event detection, Teleoperation-side aggregation and KPI computation, and Safety Services anomaly monitoring.

potential_sources: Vehicle event detection logic must be synchronized with Teleoperation clock (mitigated by NTP or GPS time sync via Network Link).
Teleoperation KPI dashboard depends on consistent Telemetry interface schema (mitigated by interface specification and versioning).
Safety Services anomaly thresholds must be coordinated with Site Manager acceptance criteria (mitigated by configuration management and stakeholder alignment).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C distributes responsibility logically: vehicle detects events (low latency, local context), Teleoperation aggregates and displays (centralized analytics, operator visibility), Safety Services monitors (safety-critical oversight). Decoupling is achieved via well-defined Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle can reliably detect load, travel, and dump events via onboard sensors (load cell, accelerometer, GPS, or operator input). Risk: Sensor noise or ambiguous state transitions may cause false event detection; mitigation: implement hysteresis and multi-sensor fusion.

Assumption: Network latency and packet loss are acceptable for near-real-time KPI reporting (e.g., 1–5 s delay). Risk: High-latency or lossy networks may degrade dashboard responsiveness; mitigation: implement local buffering and graceful degradation.

Assumption: Teleoperation system has sufficient compute capacity to aggregate and compute p95 statistics for multiple vehicles. Risk: Scalability bottleneck if fleet grows; mitigation: design for horizontal scaling or edge analytics.

Assumption: Safety Services can access Telemetry and KPI data without introducing new security or privacy concerns. Risk: Data exposure or unauthorized access; mitigation: implement role-based access control and encryption.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the event detection mechanism (sensor-based vs. operator-initiated) and define the acceptable latency budget for KPI reporting. Specify the network reliability requirements (e.g., 99.5% uptime, <5 s latency) to ensure DP-C feasibility.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the cycle-time KPI aggregation, computation, and real-time dashboard reporting. It receives vehicle event timestamps via the Telemetry interface, computes p95 statistics, and exposes KPI data to the Site Manager and Equipment Operator via the Tasking interface (outbound) and receives SafetyAlert notifications from Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sends timestamped cycle events (load_start, travel_start, dump_start, dump_end) and raw sensor data (GPS, accelerometer, load cell) to Teleoperation for KPI computation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation sends KPI dashboard updates, cycle time alerts, and performance recommendations to Site Manager and Equipment Operator consoles.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services sends anomaly alerts (e.g., cycle time exceeds safety threshold, operator fatigue detected) to Teleoperation for integration into operator dashboard.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation communicates with Vehicle and Safety Services via Network Link for telemetry ingestion, command dispatch, and alert propagation.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all subsystems and provide no architectural clarity. Not recommended.

Vehicle can perform event detection and local cycle-time logging, but KPI aggregation and dashboard reporting are operator-facing concerns outside vehicle scope. Partial containment only; diffusion risk is high if Vehicle owns the full DP.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of operator-facing KPI dashboards, real-time analytics, and performance reporting. It has direct interfaces to Site Manager and Equipment Operator. Event aggregation and p95 computation are core Teleoperation responsibilities. High containment, low diffusion.

Safety Services can monitor cycle-time anomalies and generate SafetyAlert notifications, but primary KPI reporting and dashboard ownership belong to Teleoperation. Partial containment; Safety Services is a consumer of KPI data, not the primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation is the optimal IM boundary because it owns operator-facing dashboards, has direct stakeholder interfaces (Site Manager, Equipment Operator), and is responsible for real-time analytics and performance reporting. Vehicle provides event data via Telemetry; Safety Services provides anomaly alerts via SafetyAlert. Clear separation of concerns with minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it must aggregate KPI for a large fleet of vehicles. Mitigation: design for horizontal scaling or implement edge analytics on regional gateways.

Risk: Event detection logic on Vehicle may be inconsistent or unreliable, leading to incorrect KPI computation in Teleoperation. Mitigation: implement validation and reconciliation logic in Teleoperation; log raw sensor data for post-hoc analysis.

Risk: Network latency or packet loss may cause Telemetry data to arrive out-of-order or with gaps, affecting p95 computation accuracy. Mitigation: implement time-series buffering and interpolation; define acceptable data quality thresholds.

Risk: Safety Services and Teleoperation may have conflicting thresholds or alert criteria for cycle-time anomalies. Mitigation: establish shared KPI definitions and alert policies; implement configuration management.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the event detection protocol (sensor types, thresholds, state machine) on the Vehicle side. Specify the Telemetry schema for cycle events and raw sensor data. Define the KPI computation algorithm (p95 window size, outlier handling, aggregation method) in Teleoperation. Establish interface contracts between Teleoperation and Safety Services for anomaly thresholds and alert escalation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid measurement and reporting) directly addresses the refined FR by distributing cycle-time measurement across Vehicle (event detection), Teleoperation (aggregation and KPI computation), and Safety Services (anomaly monitoring). The DP enables real-time monitoring and reporting of p95 cycle time to stakeholders. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module may accumulate multiple responsibilities (KPI computation, dashboard rendering, alert management, fleet analytics). Mitigation: implement clear internal module boundaries (e.g., KPI engine, dashboard service, alert dispatcher) and define ownership of each sub-component.

Vehicle event detection logic may be duplicated across multiple vehicle types or firmware versions. Mitigation: centralize event detection logic in a shared library or firmware module; version control and test rigorously.
Safety Services and Teleoperation may develop overlapping anomaly detection logic. Mitigation: establish a single source of truth for anomaly thresholds; implement a shared configuration service.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the primary IM owner with high containment (0.85). Vehicle provides event data (Telemetry interface); Safety Services provides alerts (SafetyAlert interface). Interfaces are well-defined and minimize coupling. Ownership boundaries are clear.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented field trials. Cycle-time measurements can be validated against ground truth (manual observation, video analysis). KPI dashboard can be inspected for correctness and responsiveness. p95 statistics can be computed offline and compared with Teleoperation output. Safety alerts can be triggered and verified for timeliness and accuracy.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle detects events and streams Telemetry; Teleoperation aggregates and computes KPI; Safety Services monitors anomalies. Each module has a well-defined responsibility. Stakeholder visibility (Site Manager, Equipment Operator) is achieved via Teleoperation dashboard. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ CT_target) can be measured and reported.

recommended_revisions: Refine the event detection protocol on Vehicle: specify sensor types (load cell, GPS, accelerometer, operator input), thresholds, and state machine logic. Document the expected accuracy and latency of event detection.
Define the Telemetry interface schema: specify the format, frequency, and reliability of cycle event messages. Include metadata (vehicle ID, timestamp, sensor confidence).

Specify the KPI computation algorithm in Teleoperation: define the p95 window size (e.g., rolling 100 cycles), outlier handling (e.g., discard cycles >2σ from mean), and aggregation method (per-vehicle, per-site, per-fleet).
Establish interface contracts between Teleoperation and Safety Services: define anomaly thresholds (e.g., cycle time >1.5× CT_target), alert escalation policies, and feedback loops.
Document the dashboard design and user experience: specify the KPI metrics displayed, update frequency, alert notifications, and drill-down capabilities for Site Manager and Equipment Operator.

decision.justification: The refined FR-2 (cycle time KPI) is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs for event detection, KPI computation, and dashboard reporting. The selected DP-C is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation (IM), but the sub-FRs (FR-2.1 through FR-2.4) must be decomposed to the next level to define detailed design parameters and implementation modules for each sub-function. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify the internal architecture of Teleoperation and define the interfaces between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: Measure and timestamp each phase of the load→travel→dump cycle with ±0.1 s accuracy.
FR-2.2: Compute p95 cycle time statistic from a rolling window of recent cycles.
FR-2.3: Report cycle time KPI and performance alerts to Site Manager and Equipment Operator via real-time dashboard.
FR-2.4: Optimize vehicle motion control and teleoperation responsiveness to minimize cycle time variance and mean latency.

assumptions: Vehicle can reliably detect load, travel, and dump events via onboard sensors or operator input.
Network latency and packet loss are acceptable for near-real-time KPI reporting (1–5 s delay).
Teleoperation system has sufficient compute capacity to aggregate and compute p95 statistics for the expected fleet size.
Safety Services can access Telemetry and KPI data without introducing new security or privacy concerns.
Site Manager and Equipment Operator have access to real-time dashboards and can act on KPI alerts within acceptable response times.
The duty cycle and site conditions are well-defined and representative of customer operations.
open_risks: Event detection reliability: sensor noise, ambiguous state transitions, or operator input errors may cause false or missed events. Mitigation: implement multi-sensor fusion, hysteresis, and validation logic.
Network reliability: high latency or packet loss may degrade KPI reporting and operator responsiveness. Mitigation: implement local buffering, graceful degradation, and network redundancy.
Scalability: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck for large fleets. Mitigation: design for horizontal scaling or edge analytics.
Data quality: inconsistent event detection across vehicle types or firmware versions may affect KPI accuracy. Mitigation: centralize event detection logic and implement rigorous testing.

Stakeholder alignment: Site Manager and Equipment Operator may have conflicting expectations for KPI targets or alert thresholds. Mitigation: establish clear acceptance criteria and communication protocols.

Safety criticality: high safety criticality of cycle-time KPI (impacts productivity and safety) requires robust monitoring and anomaly detection. Mitigation: implement Safety Services integration and define escalation policies.
evidence_needed: Detailed event detection protocol and sensor specifications for Vehicle.
Telemetry interface schema and message format definition.
KPI computation algorithm and p95 window size justification.
Dashboard design mockups and user experience specifications.
Network reliability requirements and latency budget analysis.
Scalability analysis and capacity planning for expected fleet size.
Safety Services integration plan and anomaly threshold definitions.
Field trial results validating p95 cycle time measurement accuracy and repeatability.
Stakeholder acceptance criteria and sign-off on KPI targets and alert policies.
